title: Deepfakes & Synthetic Media
concept: Deepfakes use generative AI models to swap faces or create synthetic media, requiring specialized detection models to verify authenticity.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does PII stand for in privacy engineering?
exercises.explain: PII refers to any data that can identify a specific individual, like faces or license plates.

cards.title: Believing is no longer seeing
cards.body: For over a century, a photograph was proof that something happened. Today, generative AI can create realistic videos of world leaders saying things they never said, or swap your face onto someone else's body. Welcome to the era of deepfakes!

cards.title: What is a Deepfake?
cards.body: A deepfake is synthetic media (images, video, or audio) generated by deep learning models — typically Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) or Diffusion Models — that realistically depict fake events or swap identities.

cards.title: A digital mask maker and detective
cards.body: Generating a deepfake is like a master painter painting a perfect mask over a face. Deepfake detection is like an art inspector using a magnifying glass to find tiny brush strokes, unnatural eye-blinking, or mismatched lighting that proves it's fake.

cards.title: Face-swapping GANs
cards.body: A GAN uses a generator to draw a fake face onto a video frame, and a discriminator to check if it looks real. Over millions of iterations, they improve, eventually creating a seamless face swap that fools casual viewers.

cards.title: Deepfakes have digital tells
cards.body: You might think deepfakes are perfect and undetectable. In reality, models struggle with fine-grained temporal consistency: looking closely, you can often find weird reflections in pupils, missing teeth details, or unnatural neck lines.

cards.title: Deepfake Generation Loop
cards.body: How GANs create realistic synthetic media:

left.text:
Tries to draw fake faces
Learns from errors
Optimizes pixel textures
Goal: Fool the Discriminator

right.text:
Tries to catch fake images
Compares to real photos
Flags structural glitches
Goal: Detect synthetic media

exercises.prompt: Which neural network architecture style is traditionally used to generate deepfakes by pitting a generator against a discriminator?
exercises.explain: GANs consist of two networks (generator and discriminator) competing, which drives the creation of highly realistic synthetic media.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Synthetic media refers to any digital content (images, audio, video) created or modified by AI algorithms rather than direct human recording.
exercises.explain: True. Synthetic media is the broad category for AI-generated or AI-altered content.

exercises.sentence: Looking for unnatural eye blinking or mismatched pupil reflections is a method of deepfake ___.
exercises.explain: Deepfake detection models search for subtle physical and digital anomalies to flag fake media.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a major ethical concern regarding deepfakes?

options.text: Creating fake political videos to spread misinformation and manipulate elections
Reducing the latency of mobile phone apps
Improving the accuracy of medical MRI segmentation

exercises.explain: Realistic deepfakes can easily deceive audiences, making them a powerful tool for political manipulation and fraud.

exercises.prompt: You are building a social media security system. You want to detect uploaded videos where a politician's face has been swapped onto an actor's body. What is the most effective approach?

options.text: Train a classifier to detect temporal inconsistencies, boundary artifacts, and mismatched eye-reflection vectors.
Run post-training quantization on all videos to see if they break.
Ask users to vote on whether they think the video is real.
Convert the video to black-and-white to remove color details.

exercises.explain: AI detectors look for specific synthesis anomalies like boundary blending artifacts, mismatched pupil reflections, and weird motion trajectories to identify deepfakes.
